speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, Mr. Magid. Start with your father coming in to work in the mill.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end of 1943, when the Ukrainian took over Stepan, my father start working in the mill. And he was working in the mill for a few months. And then they knew that the Russians are not far. We could hear already shooting from cannons and bombing and everything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Ukrainian, they got together. They say, we have to liquidate the rest of the Jews because we don't need no witnesses, that they're going to say that what we did with the Jews. That we killed the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one that Sasha's had a cousin. They were Baptists. So he told one of our friends a message, to tell us to hide because they're going to come tomorrow and take you and kill you. My father didn't want to believe it. And he stayed. He says, if I'll run away, they're going to kill all the Jews. The rest, there was about 50 Jews in Stepan. So next day, they picked up all the Jews and they took them to the Horyn. And there was even one was not-- not Jewish, and he had a-- he was saving a daughter and a mother. She was by us. She used to take children, I don't know what she was. When children was born, she-- I don't know what-- how to say it. She used to-- she was like a nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Midwife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Midwife, yeah. And they took them and they took the doctor, the dentist. Was maybe about 10 shoe makers and was tailors. And even they took that guy that was saving the midwife and her mother, and they took them to the Horyn. They shot them all and they threw them in in the Horyn, in the river. And we knew about it already. Then from where we were hiding, we went to his sister, about maybe 10 kilometer far. They came looking for us and he says, I don't know what happened to them. They disappeared. And, uh, about six weeks later, we got liberated from the Russians. When the Russians came in, in March 1944, it was still a lot of Ukrainian-- it was, by us, [NON-ENGLISH]. They were fighting against the Russians and against the Germans because they wanted their own country. And I knew all those guys that they-- they killed Jews. I used to take soldiers and go and look for them in the forest. And we killed a lot of them. And one of my friend, maybe about two weeks after we got liberated, says, come on, let's go too and get some of our stuff, what they rob from us, to the Ukrainian, to the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with him, and there was another guy. And while we were walking, I recognize one. He was a policeman. He shot himself, in our city, about 300 Jews. This guy was the [NON-ENGLISH]. He used to work in our place where used to make coal. He could speak Yiddish like any Jew. I mean, he was perfect in Yiddish. And he came towards us with a big coat made out of sheep skins. And I say to my friend, I say, [NON-ENGLISH], do you who it is? It's the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He was holding his hand in his pockets and coming towards us. So my friend told him stop, so he stopped. He says, take out your hand from the pockets. He took out a hand from the pockets. Pick up your hands high. When he told him to pick up the hands high, we were maybe a couple feet from each other. He took out a grenade. And he-- he wanted to pull the pin, and-- but we were very close. So the pin broke off. He threw in the grenade in his pocket back and he grabbed my friend's rifle. And they were rolling on the ground. And he took in the rifle between his legs and couldn't take it out. He was very strong. We were just after the liberation a few weeks. And I had-- we stopped before by a Ukrainian gave us a bottle of whiskey, a liter. When they were rolling, I took the bottle of whiskey and I hit him in the head and it broke on his face. And he start screaming, he can't see nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So meantime, my friend pulled the rifle out and he shot him in between his legs. When he shot him, he was laying on the floor. He was hollering, oh God, my God, are you going to kill me? So my friend tells him, you shot 300 Jews. Did you ask God, too? And we gave him two more shots and we killed him. But it was probably about 10 kilometers from the city. So we went into one of the Ukrainian to-- it was a village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we told them to give us a wagon and horses. He gave us a wagon and horses. And we took the guy and we brought him into the city. And we brought him into the city to lay him down in front of the police station. We covered him with straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they went to ask his wife, where's your husband? She told him that he's in the Russian army. And then we brought her over there to the police station. We uncovered him. And she fainted right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: At night, we took his body and we-- we took it out to the field and we buried it, not even to know where he was buried, because he was one of the worst one in our city. After that, we saw we have problems. Meantime, I received-- I had typhus. And I stayed in-- they put me in a Russian hospital. And after the typhus, I survived. Most of the people that were with me in the hospital died, but I survived. So my mother had a brother and sister in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we said, we have to get out of Russia. We have to see if we can get somehow to the United States. Then they allowed the Polish citizen from Russia, they can go to Poland. And so we had to go to a big city.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was-- the name of the city was Rovno. It was that city, at that time, lived maybe about 100,000 people. Now, I think there's more than 200,000 live there. And we registered in the Polish consulate. And then, took about maybe six or seven months, and we were immigrating to Poland. Uh, we were from-- from the Ukraine to Poland, we were-- they took us in cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father got killed when, uh, when-- when they threw him in the river. I mean, my father got killed in-- about six to eight week before we got liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: When all the people from Stepan had gone down shot by the river?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the river, yeah. That's where my father got shot, too. And when we were coming to Poland, and in Poland wasn't so hot, either. They were taking out Jews from trains, and they were shooting them. They used to be the [POLISH], that used to be the-- a Polish [POLISH] army. And they did not like Jews, either. So there was a big pogrom in Keltz. They killed, I think, maybe about 50 Jews. And so we said, we have to get out of Poland. It took a while. It was a lot of organization. They were shipping Jews to Israel and to Germany. Finally, they told us we can go over the border at night from Poland to Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia, we went to Austria. And from Austria, we went to Germany. When we were crossing the borders, they say that we are Greeks. We are not Jewish. We are Greeks. Because at that time, they let the Greek citizen out from Poland to go back to Greece because it was, at that time, I think it was a revolution in Greece or something. And when we came to Germany, they send all transport to Ulm. In Ulm, there was about five DP lagers Jewish, and there was one Polish larger for Polish non-Jews. And there was one Ukrainian lager. And we stayed in-- in Germany since '46 till 1949. In 1949, we received papers for my auntie from Detroit. And we had to go to Bremerhaven and wait for a ship to go to the United States. We came to Bremerhaven and the ship was US Marine Merlin. It was a military ship. And then-- then they loaded us up on the ship and we arrived in New York June 16, 1949. My auntie's son, his name was Bernard Weissman, he came over and he picked us up from the ship. And he took us with the train to Detroit. We arrived to Detroit on my birthday, July 17, 1949. In the beginning, my auntie had polio. She was paralyzed halfway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother was like a maid over there. And they were always complaining that we eating too much and this. We said, we can't stay here. Either or we're going back to Germany or we have to move out from there. Finally, we moved out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We rented a place on 12 Street and Joy Road on top on the market for $25 a month. And after a while, I was still going a little bit to school. And my brother was going. We were going to Northern High School on Woodward. And then I didn't even finish high school. So I figure I'm-- and they started the Korean War. I went to work for General Motors. I worked for General Motors for a while. And after that, they drafted me. They took me to the Army in 19-- the end of 1950. And they send me to basic training in Kalamazoo, Michigan. And then they send me to Alabama. Canebrake, Alabama. I was very, very sick. I couldn't-- I couldn't stand it. I stayed in the Army. I remind myself on the things what we went through during the war. And I stayed in the Army for six months. And then they dis-- discharged me on a medical discharge. So I came after they discharged me. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It was in 1951. I came back to Detroit and I went to work construction work. I was working construction work for a few years. And then I went to-- in business. We had ice cream trucks. We used to sell ice cream on the street. We had the music or bells. And we stayed in the business in Detroit till 1963. And then in 1963, we came to California. We came to California a few times, but we couldn't settle. I mean, I couldn't get a good job or make a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went three times back to Detroit. And then finally, we came, we settled here in California. After we settled in California, I start-- we organized a [INAUDIBLE] society to help Israel and to help all kind of Jewish causes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I bought here a market. I was in the market till 1980. In 1980, I was 50 years old. I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Come back. Tell me what your wife's name is. Where did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did I meet my wife? Oh, I-- I missed that. In 1956, there was a revolution in Hungary. And my wife, she ran away from there with her uncles, with her mother, with her aunties, to Austria. From Austria, they came to Detroit. Her mother had a sister that lived in Detroit from maybe 40 years. She came before the war. So when they came to Detroit, she was working at the international hot dog place on Michigan Avenue. And I had a friend, I was with him. I had a building with him together. And he says, you know, I have a nice girl for you. So he came over and he said that he's going to introduce me to her. So he introduced me to her. And she couldn't speak English. She only spoke Hungarian, but her mother spoke Jewi-- Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I liked-- I liked her and I fall in love with her. And in 1958, we got married. We got married in Detroit. We had a big wedding. We had over 200 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my oldest son was born, Joseph. And then my daughter two years later was born, Vera. And life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you go back to before the war? Let's talk about anti-Semitism. As a child, did you recognize anti-Semitism because you were so young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very, very big anti-Semitism. Because why? The Jews used to live in the city. And non-Jewish people used to live around the city. And the Jews used to dress nice. All the business and all the factories and all the nice homes and all the stores belonged to the Jewish people. Because they used to-- I mean, they used to work hard and save the money to get someplace. The Ukrainian and Polish, that's-- that's all what they used to do is drink. They used to drink. Whatever they used to make, they used to drink it up. And so they blamed everything, they blamed the Jews. Whatever was wrong, they blamed everything on the Jews. They used to say that the Jews, before Passover, they're killing Gentile kids for blood. They need blood to make matzos. And then they had-- they didn't let the Jews kill cows and-- in kosher. They had all kind of things against the Jews. And the Polish government was bought off by the Germans. They was all corrupt and all were spies in the Polish government. And so they-- they had to have a minority. They did not have any other minorities, just Jews in Poland. They had a few gypsies, but not too many. So they-- they said everything that's wrong, the Jews did it. The Jews own all the money. The Jews own all the factory. But they used to tell to the Jews, they said, the buildings are yours but the land is ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your parents being concerned that something was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We wanted to get out of there, but we did not have a way to get out. My father wanted to leave Poland right in-- after the Depression. And-- but my grandfather was-- he was very, very religious man. We had in our house, we had two kitchens. We had one for every day and one was we only used at Passover. Yeah. We had Passover dishes. Everything was separate. But-- and-- But we wanted to get out. But we had, I mean, nice homes and good business. And we even had a dacha. We used to-- my father had one building and my grandfather had one building. Every summer, we used to go to the forest where we had our dacha were there. And we used to have people coming to us during the summer to stay over the summer months, because the river was going right there and it was nice white sand. And it was a lot of fun. And you used to have all kind of organizations coming, the [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE], the-- I mean-- And they used to have like fire camps and everything else. And life was-- for the Jews, were not bad. Only then, anti-Semitism was very, very bad against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it didn't stop you. You had a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Didn't stop us. We always had-- I mean, nobody could stop. I mean, the Jews ignored it. That was the trouble. Nobody was thinking. Instead of paying for the safe, we're going to pay, we're going to pay taxes, we're going to give everything. They're not going to touch the Jews. But this was wrong. If the Jews would buy guns instead of paying for protection and fight, it would have been-- they couldn't kill half of the Jews what they killed. In Wolin, where I come from, the part was 600,000 Jews, and we survived less than 1%.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- what did you do for the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the Jewish hol-- every Friday at around two o'clock, we had a guy used to go, it's time to close the store. And everybody used to close the store. And they used to go and-- and bathe themselves and dress up nice clothes and go to shul. Every store on Saturday was closed. There was not even one store open on Saturday. Even non-Jewish stores weren't open on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father employ non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Most of them was non-Jews that worked in the mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have friends as a child--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish. I mean, it was no different to us. I mean, we were getting along very well. But later when-- when they told them to-- to take everything away from the Jews and to kill, our best friends were our worst enemies. What they were eating in our house, what they were drinking in our house, what we helped them. They were our worst enemies. We had a maid that she was by us maybe more than 10 years. She ate by us. She slept by us. She only had a mother, and the mother used to carry water to the houses. And they were very poor. So my grandparents took them in, and she used to take care of the kids and clean and wash. And when the Germans came, she was the first one they came. She knew where everything was because she lived in our house. She took away the gold, the silver. My grandfather had a silver megillah for Purim, and she took it away, too. And my grandfather says, [INAUDIBLE]. Why you-- we were so nice to you and everything. Says, you don't need that. They're going to kill you anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grand-- grand-- my grandfather died just maybe a month before the liquidation of the ghetto. They killed my-- they killed my three uncles. They killed my auntie. They killed my grandmother. And I had my-- two of my uncles had two children, each one, two boys. They killed them and their wives. And they killed my mother's mother, my grandmother from my mother's side. I heard they clubbed her to death with-- with-- with wood. With-- they clubbed her to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the early '40s, did you know what was going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not-- there was no news. Nobody knew what's going on. We knew there was a war going on, but nobody knew that they're killing all the Jews. I mean, it was very secret-- done secretly. We-- we figure that something is wrong, but they say, no, no. Here, they're not going to kill. Because there must have did something, the Jews that they killed someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know the vast numbers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you familiar with transports that were going places?

speaker: interviewee
text: From us, they wasn't transporting. From the Ukraine, they did not transfer. They used to take them out. Used to be ditches and everybody has to undress. Used to line up five in a row, and through the neck, they used to shoot five people. They were saving bullets. So maybe they shot one or two, but three used to fall in alive. And they buried. The kids, they didn't even shoot. The kids, they threw them in-- in the-- in the ditches. And then-- and they buried them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've seen Germans were making practice, shooting practice on-- on little kids. They used to throw a kid, and a German used to shoot. Three, four, five months old kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- how many people-- during the liquidation, how many people fled?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in our ghetto, it was 3,000 people, but 500 fled. But life was very bad. Nobody wanted to help. Nobody wanted to give a piece of bread, even. So mostly, they caught them and they shot every-- most of them. And it was-- from our place, we survived 36 people. My mother was the only woman to survive. The rest of them was all 35 boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was your mother the only woman that survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she knew good Ukrainian and she used to know a lot of Ukrainian people. And she used to go beg from them. And she was the only one that survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you spoke about when you were in someone's barn, how did you have the money to pay for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, by the lot of the Ukrainian, we had hidden stuff. And plus, we had buried a lot of stuff in certain places. And we used to send the guy and to the people, and they used to give him-- give him the-- we had coats. We had shirts. We had pants. We had gold. We had silver. We had even dollars. We had what the auntie used to send us, sometimes $20, $10 from the United States. So we had buried a lot of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you slept, when you said about potatoes not freezing, were you actually in the dirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the earth?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was in the ground. Yeah. It was dirt. Dirt. We only had a little straw on the bottom. So I mean, not to sleep on the dirt. But it was dirt. We-- we lived there almost a year underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just look out. We had-- made a look out. We couldn't go out because if somebody-- Sometimes I used to go out in the forest to bake a potato or something. But one time I went out, a guy was coming to see the girls, the guy what he was hiding us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his father was a policeman, a murderer. Who knows how many Jews his father shot? So he wanted to-- to take me to the police. So that guy, it was a cousin of his. He says, no, don't take him. He's-- he's only hiding here.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's helping me with taking care on the cows and on the pigs. So he didn't tell nobody. But I think that guy later must have told his father. That's why they burned down the barn while we were there. If he wouldn't leave, we wouldn't be alive. We would be burned in the barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any kind of religious observance while you were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't even know when the holidays or anything. We didn't know what day-- I mean, we knew it was Monday. But that's about it. But we didn't know the dates or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever he used to give us. And my mother or my father used to go back to at night to people and bring some piece of bread, crumbs, raw potatoes. I used to have in my stomach live worms.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
